title: Share of global cumulative CO₂ emissions from land-use change
subtitle: Emissions from [land-use change](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) can be positive or negative depending on whether these changes emit (positive) or sequester (negative) carbon. Cumulative emissions are the running sum of emissions since 1850.
caption:
Share of global cumulative CO₂ emissions from land-use change

Emissions from land-use change can be positive or negative depending on whether these changes emit (positive) or sequester (negative) carbon. Cumulative emissions are the running sum of emissions since 1850.

This choropleth world map displays the share of global cumulative CO₂ emissions from land-use change as of 2024, with data representing the running sum of emissions since 1850. The legend uses a color scale ranging from teal for negative values (sequestration) through pale yellow for near-zero shares, up to dark brown for shares exceeding 10%. The United States, Brazil, and Russia occupy the darkest brown band, indicating shares greater than 10% of the global cumulative total. China, Indonesia, and India appear in a slightly lighter but still dark orange-brown band, corresponding to shares between 5% and 10%. Canada, Australia, and several countries in South America and Southeast Asia sit in the mid-range orange shades, falling between the 2% and 5% thresholds. Much of Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Eastern Europe display lighter yellow and pale orange shades, placing them in the 0.2% to 2% range. France and a few other European nations appear in teal, signifying negative cumulative emissions from land-use change over this period. Greenland and Antarctica are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The visual distribution shows the highest cumulative shares concentrated in large landmass nations across the Americas and Eurasia, while many smaller nations and regions in Africa and the Middle East hold smaller shares of the global total.
